title: Fracture and suspected broken bone first aid
text: A suspected fracture or broken bone can cause severe pain, swelling, bruising, bleeding, deformity, numbness, tingling, limited movement, or inability to bear weight. Open fractures, severe bleeding, loss of circulation, blue or cold skin, head neck back hip pelvis or upper leg injury, and visible bone require urgent emergency care. First aid includes keeping the person still, covering wounds, applying cold packs through a cloth, elevating when safe, immobilizing above and below the injury, and avoiding attempts to straighten or test the bone.

title: Common cold self-care and antibiotic safety
text: The common cold is usually a viral upper respiratory infection with runny nose, congestion, sore throat, cough, sneezing, mild headache, and low fever. Most people recover with rest, fluids, humidified air, saline spray, honey for adults and children over one year, and over-the-counter symptom relief when safe. Antibiotics do not treat viral colds and unnecessary antibiotics can cause side effects and antimicrobial resistance. Seek medical care for trouble breathing, chest pain, dehydration, symptoms that worsen or last unusually long, high fever, or risk factors such as immune compromise.

title: Fever triage and symptom relief
text: Fever is an elevated body temperature and can occur with infections, inflammatory illness, heat exposure, and other conditions. Supportive care can include fluids, rest, light clothing, and fever reducing medicines such as acetaminophen or ibuprofen when safe for the person. Children and teenagers should not use aspirin unless a clinician specifically instructs it. Emergency evaluation is needed for severe symptoms such as confusion, stiff neck, breathing difficulty, persistent vomiting, dehydration, seizure, rash with fever, or fever in a very young infant.

title: NSAID safety warnings
text: Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs, also called NSAIDs, include ibuprofen, naproxen, and aspirin-containing products. NSAIDs can increase the risk of stomach bleeding and can increase risk of heart attack or stroke, especially in people with heart disease, high blood pressure, kidney disease, stomach ulcer or bleeding history, blood thinners, older age, pregnancy, heavy alcohol use, or prolonged use. Warning signs include vomiting blood, black or bloody stools, severe abdominal pain, chest pain, shortness of breath, weakness on one side, or slurred speech. Medicine decisions should consider allergies, age, pregnancy, kidney disease, liver disease, and current medicines.

title: Allergic reaction and anaphylaxis red flags
text: Allergic reactions can include rash, itching, swelling, hives, sneezing, watery eyes, vomiting, or wheezing. Anaphylaxis is a severe allergic reaction with airway swelling, trouble breathing, wheezing, fainting, rapid worsening, throat tightness, or swelling of lips tongue or face. Anaphylaxis is an emergency and requires immediate emergency care. People with known severe allergy may be prescribed epinephrine auto-injectors by a clinician. Avoid suspected triggers and do not retry a medicine or food that caused severe symptoms without medical advice.

title: Chest pain emergency triage
text: Chest pain can come from heart, lung, muscle, digestive, anxiety, or other causes. Emergency care is needed for chest pressure, crushing pain, pain spreading to arm jaw back or shoulder, shortness of breath, sweating, nausea, fainting, irregular heartbeat, weakness, or chest pain with risk factors such as heart disease. Do not drive yourself to emergency care if serious heart symptoms are possible.

title: Vomiting diarrhea and dehydration care
text: Vomiting and diarrhea are often caused by viral gastroenteritis, foodborne illness, medicine side effects, or other infection. The main risk is dehydration. Oral rehydration solution, small frequent sips, bland foods when tolerated, and rest can help. Seek care for blood in stool or vomit, severe abdominal pain, signs of dehydration, confusion, very high fever, persistent vomiting, pregnancy, infants, older adults, or immune compromise.

title: Headache and migraine red flags
text: Headache can be tension-type, migraine, sinus-related, medicine-related, dehydration-related, or due to serious neurologic disease. Emergency red flags include sudden worst headache, headache after head injury, fever with stiff neck, confusion, seizure, fainting, weakness, vision loss, new severe headache during pregnancy, or new headache in a person with cancer or immune compromise. Self-care for non-emergency headache can include fluids, rest, avoiding triggers, and safe over-the-counter pain relief.

title: Wounds and infection warning signs
text: Cuts, scrapes, punctures, burns, and wounds need cleaning, protection, and monitoring. Warning signs of infection include increasing redness, warmth, swelling, worsening pain, pus, fever, red streaks, or delayed healing. Deep wounds, animal bites, dirty punctures, heavy bleeding, numbness, loss of function, burns, or wounds in people with diabetes or immune compromise need medical evaluation.

title: Asthma symptoms and flare safety
text: Asthma is a chronic airway condition that can cause wheezing, coughing, chest tightness, and shortness of breath. Symptoms may worsen with smoke, dust, exercise, infections, cold air, or allergens. Severe breathlessness, blue lips, confusion, exhaustion, or poor response to a prescribed reliever inhaler needs urgent care. People with asthma should follow a clinician-provided action plan.

title: Pneumonia and lower respiratory infection warning signs
text: Pneumonia can cause cough, fever, chills, breathing difficulty, chest discomfort, fatigue, and sometimes confusion in older adults. Medical evaluation is important for fast breathing, low oxygen, chest pain, coughing blood, older age, pregnancy, immune compromise, or symptoms that worsen. Treatment depends on the cause and may require clinician-prescribed medicines.

title: Urinary tract infection symptoms and escalation
text: Urinary tract infections can cause burning urination, frequent urination, urgency, cloudy or bloody urine, lower abdominal pain, and strong-smelling urine. Fever, back or flank pain, vomiting, pregnancy, diabetes, kidney disease, male sex, or recurrent symptoms need medical advice because infection can spread to the kidneys.

title: Diabetes high and low blood sugar symptoms
text: Diabetes affects blood sugar regulation. High blood sugar can cause thirst, frequent urination, fatigue, blurred vision, slow healing, or weight change. Low blood sugar can cause sweating, shakiness, hunger, confusion, weakness, dizziness, or fainting. Severe confusion, vomiting, deep breathing, fainting, or ketones with high readings requires urgent medical care.

title: High blood pressure and emergency symptoms
text: High blood pressure often has no symptoms, but repeated high readings require clinician follow-up. Very high blood pressure with chest pain, breathing difficulty, weakness, confusion, severe headache, vision change, or neurologic symptoms may be a hypertensive emergency and needs urgent care. Do not change prescribed medicines without clinician guidance.

title: Back pain red flags and self-care
text: Back pain often improves with time, gentle activity, posture care, and comfort measures. Red flags include major trauma, fever, cancer history, unexplained weight loss, leg weakness, numbness in the groin area, loss of bladder or bowel control, or inability to pass urine. These signs require urgent medical evaluation.

title: Anxiety and panic symptoms
text: Anxiety and panic can cause intense fear, racing heart, fast breathing, chest tightness, dizziness, tingling, sweating, and trembling. New or severe chest pain, fainting, severe shortness of breath, or neurologic symptoms should not be assumed to be anxiety without medical evaluation.

title: Depression and self-harm risk
text: Depression can include persistent sadness, loss of interest, sleep or appetite changes, guilt, low energy, poor concentration, or thoughts of death or self-harm. Active suicidal thoughts or self-harm risk is an emergency and requires immediate support from emergency services, a crisis line, or a trusted person.

title: Anemia symptoms and causes
text: Anemia means the body does not have enough healthy red blood cells or hemoglobin. Symptoms can include fatigue, weakness, dizziness, shortness of breath with exertion, pale skin, headache, or fast heartbeat. Causes include iron deficiency, blood loss, vitamin deficiency, chronic disease, and other conditions. Testing is needed to know the cause.

title: Sinusitis and nasal congestion
text: Sinusitis or sinus congestion can cause facial pressure, blocked nose, thick nasal drainage, cough, headache, and reduced smell. Many cases follow viral colds or allergies. Severe facial swelling, vision changes, high fever, severe headache, or symptoms that worsen or last unusually long should be evaluated.

title: Ear infection and ear pain warning signs
text: Ear infections and ear pain can cause earache, fever, hearing reduction, fluid drainage, fussiness in children, or balance symptoms. Severe pain, swelling around the ear, discharge, hearing loss, fever, young children, or recurrent symptoms need clinician evaluation. Do not insert objects into the ear canal.

title: Pink eye and red eye safety
text: Conjunctivitis can cause red, itchy, watery, crusted, or discharge-producing eyes. Causes include viruses, bacteria, allergies, irritation, and contact lens problems. Eye pain, vision change, injury, severe light sensitivity, chemical exposure, or contact lens-related redness requires urgent eye care.

title: GERD heartburn and chest pain caution
text: Gastroesophageal reflux disease can cause heartburn, sour taste, burping, regurgitation, cough, or throat irritation. Chest pain can also come from heart disease, so crushing chest pain, pain spreading to arm jaw back or shoulder, sweating, fainting, or shortness of breath requires emergency care.

title: Dehydration and heat illness
text: Dehydration can cause thirst, dry mouth, dizziness, low urination, dark urine, weakness, confusion, and fast heartbeat. Vomiting, diarrhea, fever, heavy sweating, heat exposure, infants, older adults, and chronic illness raise risk. Confusion, fainting, no urination, inability to drink, or heat stroke signs need urgent care.

title: COVID-19 and viral respiratory symptoms
text: COVID-19 and other viral respiratory illnesses can cause fever, cough, sore throat, runny nose, body aches, fatigue, headache, loss of smell or taste, nausea, vomiting, or diarrhea. Emergency warning signs include trouble breathing, persistent chest pain or pressure, confusion, inability to wake, pale gray or blue skin lips or nail beds, dehydration, or worsening symptoms.

title: Fungal infection imported disease profile
text: Disease: Fungal infection. Description: In humans, fungal infections occur when an invading fungus takes over an area of the body and is too much for the immune system to handle.
